summary: Full Stack Web Developer with over 3 years of experience in developing software products, specializing in both front-end and back-end development. Highly proficient in JavaScript, and experienced in building scalable, optimized, and maintainable software. Skilled in working both independently and collaboratively, with a focus on writing clean, efficient, and reusable code.

responsibilities: Developed SaaS applications and trading bots using NestJS, Node.js, TypeScript, PostgreSQL, and Google Cloud Functions.
Implemented SMS auto-subscription, alerts, and messaging workflows using Twilio and MessageBird.
Integrated Stripe for payments and subscription management.
Built and deployed scalable backend services on Railway, leveraging Airtable for data and automation.
Contributed to project planning, system design, and ongoing codebase refactoring.
Led projects and worked on end-to-end development using technologies such as React, Next.js, Node.js, Nest.js, GraphQL, Typescript, Prisma ORM, and Sequelize.
Developed both frontend and backend components, working on data integration and API development with Clerk, Passport.js, and Firebase Authentication.
Built responsive, user-friendly applications using PrimeReact, Formik, Tailwind CSS, and Redux Toolkit.
Refactored existing designs and ensured code reusability and maintainability.
Implemented new features and fixed bugs on both the front-end and back-end of various projects.
Developed APIs using Node.js, Express.js, and MongoDB, and collaborated with front-end developers to integrate UI elements.
Worked within an Agile scrum environment and used Git for version control.
Scraped data from social media and other websites for integration into applications.

projects.title: SMS-Auto - Community Weft

Built Stripe checkout and webhook integrations for subscriptions and one-time payments with phone pre-filling
Integrated Airtable for payment and phone-record persistence with tier updates during unsubscribe flows
Automated monthly care-message delivery and post-payment welcome message sequences

projects.description: Built a professional TypeScript-based arbitrage detection and execution bot that connects to Kalshi and Polymarket, identifies cross-platform opportunities with fee/gas-aware net edge calculations, and supports both paper and live trading.
achievements: Implemented market normalization and mapping across two prediction platforms with realistic fee and gas models
Built arbitrage detection logic with net-edge filtering and dynamic execution intervals (5-minute base, 1-minute final hour)

projects.title: Mail Flow Agent

Implemented attachment storage with partnership-based folders, inferred document types (BS / IS / RR / TB), and checklist updates to tblQuarterlyCheckList
Delivered a FastAPI admin panel for operational settings with CSRF protection, settings history, run dashboards, and secure separation of secrets in .env
Added configurable rules (email filters, confidence thresholds, mark-as-read only when saved) and structured logging with run summaries and DB health checks

projects.title: Growth Genie (Web + API + FastAPI)
projects.description: Full-stack growth platform: a Next.js App Router client for idea validation, AI success scoring, expert-style feedback, auth (login, OTP, password reset), dashboards, knowledge base, file uploads with Cloudinary, PDF handling, voice input, and real-time insight flows; Redis powers live persona working status, paired with a NestJS API and FastAPI services for execution and resume-related workflows.
achievements: Built the Growth Genie web app with idea validation, AI-powered success scores, persona-based analysis, and hypothesis testing with visual analytics
Implemented authentication, dashboard insights, knowledge base, Cloudinary-backed uploads, PDF-to-image handling, and speech-driven input
Contributed to the NestJS backend API and FastAPI services for execution flows and resume submission features integrated from the web client
Implemented Redis-backed real-time status for expert personas (working/idle) and pushed updates to the UI via WebSocket, alongside structured NestJS and FastAPI layers

achievements: Integrated real-time order notifications and barcode scanner to verify age by USA driving licence
Implemented order tracking to ensure smooth fulfillment and customer satisfaction
Integrated Twilio for SMS and email notifications to keep customers and store staff updated
Data management handled using MongoDB for scalability and performance

Used OpenAI API on the front end to detect and classify health-conscious products

Collaborated with backend and data teams to ensure smooth flow of filtered data from 2.5M+ Shopify stores

Re-engineered front-end components using Vue.js, Pinia and Quasar to align with updated backend logic
Ensured smooth integration with .NET-based APIs and improved responsiveness across devices
Contributed to overall performance optimization and UI consistency in a complex enterprise environment

projects.title: E-Commerce Website with Next.js, Nest.js, and MySQL
projects.description: Built a full-featured e-commerce website using Next.js for the frontend, Nest.js for the backend, and MySQL as the database.
achievements: Implemented efficient state management using Redux Toolkit to manage user data, cart information, and product details
Integrated GraphQL for streamlined and optimized data fetching between the frontend and backend
Designed and developed the frontend with a modern and responsive user interface powered by Tailwind CSS
Utilized Sequelize ORM for MySQL database interaction, ensuring smooth data handling and optimization
Implemented user authentication, order management, and payment systems

Integrated TypeORM with SQL Server for backend data handling and dynamic content management
Implemented user authentication with Passport.js and secured APIs for seamless access to portfolio data
Used React Hook Form for form management, allowing potential clients to request more information
Made the website fully responsive with Tailwind CSS, ensuring smooth user experience across all devices

projects.title: Pakistan Petrol Price Mobile App - Backend API
projects.description: Developed the backend API for the Pakistan Petrol Price mobile application, which has garnered over 300K downloads.
achievements: Used LoopBack 4 to create a secure and scalable API that serves real-time petrol price data to mobile clients
Integrated Puppeteer for scraping data on petrol prices from external sources for up-to-date information
Incorporated Google Maps API to help users find nearby petrol stations and calculate fuel efficiency
Added user authentication for data security and personalized features like trip tracking and service reminders

projects.description: Built the backend for the Pakistani Brands Mobile App, which aggregates data from 50+ top Pakistani apparel websites.

Integrated OneSignal to send push notifications to users about sales and new products
Stored and managed data in SQL Server using Sequelize ORM for smooth data retrieval
Built a secure API with LoopBack 4 and TypeScript for managing user interactions and product listings

projects.title: Currency Converter App Backend
projects.description: Developed the backend for a Currency Converter App, supporting real-time currency conversion for 20 countries.
achievements: Integrated with external APIs to fetch live exchange rates of top currencies
Added daily updates for gold prices in major Pakistani cities for comprehensive financial data

Implemented OneSignal for sending push notifications about currency and gold price updates
Implemented secure user authentication with Passport.js for personalized services

blogs.title: Production-Grade APIs with NestJS, PostgreSQL & Prisma
blogs.excerpt: How I structure modules, migrations, and transactional boundaries when shipping SaaS backends that need predictable behavior under load.
takeaways: Domain-oriented modules beat “technical layer” folders when products evolve weekly
Treat Prisma migrations as the contract between services and BI tooling
Model side-effects (webhooks, queues) as explicit steps out of the request path
paragraphs: When you are shipping billing, alerts, and automation in one codebase, the dominant risk is not peak RPS—it is ambiguity about who owns a workflow. I start by naming bounded contexts (subscriptions, messaging, integrations) and keep providers thin so tests stay honest.

PostgreSQL gives you the constraints you need for money-moving paths; Prisma makes the schema legible to the whole team. I default to explicit transactions for anything that touches balances, tiers, or ledger-like tables, and I document failure modes beside the use-case—not in a wiki nobody opens.

The “premium” feel for API consumers is consistency: predictable error shapes, idempotent handlers, and observability that answers why a webhook retry happened. That trio costs little upfront and pays for itself the first night you debug production.

blogs.title: Next.js & React Patterns for Interfaces That Feel Instant
blogs.excerpt: Streaming, server-first data, and client islands—how I keep dashboards and marketing surfaces fast without sacrificing interactivity.
takeaways: Fetch on the server when the UI is mostly read-only; hydrate only the interactive islands
Co-locate loading and error UI so perceived performance wins even when networks wobble
Let React Query own cache policy for anything that mutates in real time
paragraphs: Users judge quality in milliseconds. I reach for App Router layouts when the page shell is stable but the inner widgets need motion, forms, or charts—Framer Motion and React Flow stay on the client while the document frame stays lean.

Tailwind is not just styling speed; it is a shared vocabulary with design. Pair it with small component primitives (inputs, tables, dialogs) and you avoid the “custom CSS sprawl” that makes premium products feel cheap over time.
The finishing touches—focus rings, reduced-motion respect, and intentional empty states—are what separate a demo from a product people trust.

blogs.title: WebSockets, Redis & Backpressure in Live Dashboards
blogs.excerpt: Patterns for persona status, bot telemetry, and trading-adjacent UIs where stale data is worse than no data.
takeaways: Separate transport (socket) from domain events so you can swap brokers later
Use Redis as a fast source of truth for presence, not as your primary ledger
Log structured cycles—your future self is debugging at 2am, not admiring architecture diagrams
paragraphs: Live interfaces need a story for fan-out: who listens, what they are allowed to see, and how you degrade when a client falls behind. I like Redis-backed presence for short-lived flags and a clear TTL story.

Backpressure is the hidden premium feature. If you push firehoses to browsers, you will thrash layouts and batteries. Batch, throttle, and surface “catching up” states with the same care as loading spinners.
Pair operational logs (Winston) with user-visible timelines so support and engineering share one narrative when something misbehaves.

blogs.title: Stripe Webhooks, Idempotency Keys & Safe Subscription Flows
blogs.excerpt: Lessons from SMS-based acquisition flows—making payments feel boring in the best way.
takeaways: Treat every webhook as at-least-once delivery—keys and deduplication tables are non-negotiable
Persist Stripe customer ↔ user mapping early; retro-matching is painful
Test failure replay as aggressively as happy paths
paragraphs: Payments are trust interfaces. A premium checkout is not flashy animation—it is correct receipts, predictable proration behavior, and support teams that can explain state in one glance.
I store webhook envelopes with status, attempt counts, and correlation IDs. Debugging becomes reading a story instead of guessing race conditions.
When SMS or messaging is in the loop, time ordering gets weird. Idempotency and explicit state machines for keywords like STOP/LOVE/UNSUB keep you compliant and calm.

blogs.title: GraphQL in the Wild with Prisma, NestJS & Product Dashboards
blogs.excerpt: When GraphQL wins, how I avoid N+1 nightmares, and where I still default to REST.
takeaways: Batch loaders belong in the architecture from day one—not static import({}) tricks
Model errors so mobile and web clients can branch without string matching
Reach for REST for binaries, caching-unfriendly payloads, and third-party callbacks
paragraphs: GraphQL shines when product teams iterate on screens faster than endpoints can multiply. The cost is discipline: schemas, permissions, and performance budgets must be first-class.
Prisma’s query planner is good, but GraphQL resolvers can still amplify chatter. DataLoader-style batching is the difference between slick UX and mysteriously slow grids.
Premium API surfaces expose introspection thoughtfully in dev, harden in prod, and document the “why” next to every expensive field.

blogs.title: Docker, PM2 & Practical Node Deployments That Stay Boring
blogs.excerpt: Checklists for bots, workers, and APIs—where immutability meets operational comfort.
takeaways: One artifact per release—configure via env, not secret commits
Health checks should validate dependencies, not just `200 OK` on `/`
Use PM2 or orchestrators for restart policy; hope is not a supervision strategy
paragraphs: Shipping trading-adjacent or messaging-heavy services means processes will crash, deploys will overlap, and disks will fill. Container images plus explicit process managers turn chaos into playbooks.
Nginx fronts TLS and static egress concerns; Node focuses on business logic. Premium operations means rotations, logging sinks, and dashboards that show saturation before users notice.
The goal is unexciting on-call: predictable rollbacks, obvious config diffs, and alerts that fire on symptoms—not ego metrics.

learningHub.headline: Learning space
learningHub.subhead: Curated tracks grounded in the same stack I ship with—full-stack TypeScript, resilient APIs, realtime dashboards, and cloud-native delivery.

tracks.title: Product-grade full-stack
tracks.summary: End-to-end delivery from Next.js surfaces to NestJS services and PostgreSQL.

steps.title: Type-safe contracts first
steps.detail: Align Zod/DTO layers with OpenAPI or generated clients so your UI and API evolve together.

steps.title: Auth as a product feature
steps.detail: Map sessions, OAuth2 flows, and webhook identities to explicit user states—not implicit flags.

steps.title: Ship vertical slices
steps.detail: Prefer user stories that touch UI, API, and persistence over horizontal “frontend week” milestones.

tracks.title: Frontend excellence
tracks.summary: Interfaces that feel premium: motion, accessibility, and data discipline.

steps.title: Design systems in Tailwind
steps.detail: Tokens, spacing rhythm, and component recipes keep React trees consistent at scale.

steps.title: State with intent
steps.detail: Redux Toolkit for app-wide workflows; React Query for remote cache; Context for scoped toggles only.

steps.title: Complex viz without jank
steps.detail: Isolate charts and graphs (Chart.js, React Flow) behind memoized shells with stable data snapshots.

tracks.title: Backend & platform
tracks.summary: Messaging, integrations, and observability for serious traffic.

steps.title: Integrations that recover
steps.detail: Twilio, Stripe, and MessageBird demand retries, idempotency, and dead-letter clarity.

steps.title: Postgres as the system of record
steps.detail: Use constraints and migrations boldly; let caches (Redis) be accelerators—not sources of truth.

steps.title: Serverless glue with guardrails
steps.detail: Google Cloud Functions for focused jobs; keep shared libraries versioned and tested locally first.

tracks.title: AI-assisted automation
tracks.summary: When to blend Azure OpenAI, Microsoft Graph, and FastAPI for operational wins.

steps.title: Inbox-to-action pipelines
steps.detail: Graph API ingestion, attachment routing, and human-in-the-loop review for regulated content.

steps.title: Confidence-aware matching
steps.detail: Layer exact, fuzzy, and model-based strategies with explicit thresholds and audit trails.

steps.title: Operational UX for agents
steps.detail: Admin panels with history, rollback, and settings snapshots keep AI features maintainable.
